content_sections.heading: Pharmacology
content_sections.body: Adrenaline is a sympathomimetic catecholamine with alpha and beta adrenergic effects. Alpha effects cause peripheral vasoconstriction, increasing systemic vascular resistance and diastolic blood pressure. Beta-1 effects increase heart rate and myocardial contractility. Beta-2 effects cause bronchodilation and skeletal muscle vasodilation.

content_sections.heading: Dose Recommendations
content_sections.body:
Anaphylaxis: 0.5 mg IM (0.5 mL of 1:1000 solution) into mid-anterolateral thigh. May repeat every 5 minutes if required.

Cardiac arrest: 1 mg IV/IO (10 mL of 1:10000 solution) every 3-5 minutes.

Severe asthma: 0.5 mg IM, may repeat.

Hypotension/shock: Commence infusion at 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min and titrate to response.

content_sections.heading: Contraindications
content_sections.body: There are no absolute contraindications to adrenaline in life-threatening situations such as cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis. In less urgent situations, use with caution in patients with hypertension, tachyarrhythmias, or narrow-angle glaucoma.

content_sections.heading: Precautions
content_sections.body: Adrenaline should be used with caution in pregnancy, though the benefits in life-threatening situations outweigh risks. Monitor ECG and blood pressure during administration. Extravasation can cause tissue necrosis - ensure secure IV access.
